figures.caption: Figure 4. Ten realizations of r̂t(1) and r̂t(0) obtained by resampling treatment indicators wi, compared with true values rt(1) and rt(0), for Dataset 5.1.

Figure 1. A single realization from Dataset 5.1 comparing classical pointwise confidence intervals (dark shaded) from equation (28) with the anytime-valid confidence processes from Theorems 4.6 and 4.9 (light shaded). Left panel: cumulative rewards rt(w) and estimates r̂t(w) for control (grey) and treatment (blue), showing the “pull-forward” effect where treatment outcomes arrive earlier but control accumulates higher reward over time. Right panel: treatment effect ∆t and estimate ∆̂t. Red dots indicate times where the pointwise interval fails to cover the truth.

Figure 3. Illustration of outcome dependence on treatment, internal time, and external time for Dataset 5.1. The potential outcome yi(w) depends on treatment w (which determines the baseline βw), internal time si = ti(w)− Ei (time since entry, contributing a logarithmic growth), and external time ti(w) (contributing cyclical variation).

Figure 2. Sample paths of estimation errors from 100 Monte Carlo replications of Dataset 5.1. Left panel: r̂t(0)− rt(0) vs. variance time Vt(0), with confidence bands ±b(Vt(0);α/2). Center panel: r̂t(1) − rt(1) vs. variance time Vt(1), with confidence bands ±b(Vt(1);α/2). Right panel: ∆̂t −∆t vs. variance time σ2 t , with confidence bands ± (b(Vt(0);α/2) + b(Vt(1);α/2)) (green) and ±b(σ2 t ;α) (red).

Figure 6. Design-based confidence sequences for OOM error counts. Left: single-arm confidence sequences for rt(0) and rt(1). Center: treatment effect confidence sequence for ∆t = rt(1)− rt(0). Right: sequential p-value for testing H0 : ∆t = 0.

Figure 7. Poisson process confidence sequences for the same data, scaled by 1/π(w) = 2 to target the same estimand rt(w) as the design-based approach. Left: confidence sequences for rt(0) and rt(1). Center: confidence sequence for ∆t = rt(1)− rt(0). Right: sequential p-value. The point estimates are identical to Figure 6; the confidence intervals differ only due to the distinct boundary functions used by each method.
Figure 5. Example of martingale violation for Cov(∆̂s, ∆̂t) ̸= Var(∆̂s) for Dataset 5.1, using random augmentation terms mit(0) iid∼ Uniform(0.5, 2.0) and mit(1) iid∼ Uniform(0.2, 1.0).
